content.value: A plain BST can degenerate into a linked list (e.g. inserting already-sorted data), making operations O(n) instead of O(log n). An AVL tree (Adelson-Velsky and Landis) is a self-balancing BST that fixes this by keeping every subtree's height difference small.

For every node, the balance factor = height(left subtree) - height(right subtree). An AVL tree requires this value to stay in {-1, 0, 1} for every node. Whenever an insertion or deletion pushes a node's balance factor outside this range, a rotation restores it.

items: Left-Right (LR): left rotation on child, then right rotation on node
Right-Left (RL): right rotation on child, then left rotation on node
1. Insert the node exactly as in a normal BST
3. At each ancestor, compute the balance factor
4. If unbalanced, identify which of the four cases applies (by comparing keys) and apply the corresponding rotation(s)

content.value: Identify the rotation case by checking where the newly inserted key falls relative to the unbalanced node and its child: if it went left-left, it's LL; left then right, it's LR; and so on.
Why AVL trees are worth the overhead

By guaranteeing height stays O(log n), AVL trees keep search, insert, and delete all at O(log n) in every case - unlike a plain BST, which can degrade to O(n). The cost is the extra bookkeeping (heights/balance factors) and occasional rotations on every insert/delete.

Why use an AVL tree instead of a plain BST?
options: It guarantees O(log n) height, avoiding worst-case O(n) operations
